meta.purpose: GraphRAG seed — maps OAS standards to the 10-Track Constitution for curriculum routing in adeline-brain
meta.maintainer: adeline-core/src/types.ts → Track enum is the authority

mappings.subject: English Language Arts
mappings.standard_text: Demonstrate understanding of the organization and basic features of print: follow words from left to right, top to bottom, and page by page; recognize that spoken words are represented in written language; understand that words are separated by spaces in print.
mappings.rationale: Print awareness is the foundation of literacy — the entry point for all truth-seeking and primary source work in Adeline.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: When you look at a page, where do you start reading? Why does that matter?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Practice reading seed packet labels and farm sign lettering from left to right, top to bottom.

mappings.subject: Mathematics
mappings.standard_text: Count to 100 by ones and by tens. Count forward beginning from a given number within the known sequence.
mappings.rationale: Counting is the first act of mathematics — the foundation of measurement, commerce, and creation order.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Can you count the eggs in the basket? Now count by tens — how many groups of ten is that?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Count eggs, seeds, or fence posts on the farm. Group them by tens to practice skip counting in a real context.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Counting animals, plants, and produce is applied arithmetic

mappings.subject: Science
mappings.standard_text: Observe, describe, and compare the basic needs of living things — plants and animals need food, water, air, and space to survive.
mappings.rationale: The kindergarten creation entry point — all life shares basic needs because one Creator designed the system.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: What does every living thing need? Did God design that on purpose?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Water the garden with your child and discuss what each plant needs. Observe animals and compare their needs to ours.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Farm stewardship depends on understanding living things' needs

mappings.subject: Social Studies
mappings.standard_text: Distinguish between past and present; describe how things change over time using words like before, after, yesterday, today, and tomorrow.
mappings.rationale: Time vocabulary is the kindergarten foundation for all historical reasoning — you can't read primary sources without knowing what 'long ago' means.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: What happened yesterday? What is happening today? History is just the story of what happened before now.
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Look at old and new photos of the farm. What changed? What stayed the same?

mappings.subject: Health
mappings.standard_text: Identify basic healthy behaviors that affect personal health, including eating a variety of foods, drinking water, getting enough sleep, and washing hands.
mappings.rationale: The body as a created gift — kindergartners learn that caring for it is an act of gratitude and stewardship.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: God made your body — how do we take care of something that belongs to God?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Make a healthy snack from the garden together and talk about where the food came from and why it's good for your body.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Growing your own food is a foundational health practice

mappings.subject: English Language Arts
mappings.standard_text: Locate and use text features (e.g., title, headings, captions, bold print, glossaries, indexes, table of contents) to understand informational texts.
mappings.rationale: Builds foundational literacy skills — navigating informational structure is the gateway to all truth-seeking in Adeline.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: How do titles and headings help us know what a book is really about?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Read seed packet labels, canning jar lids, and farm almanac headings as real informational texts.

mappings.subject: Science
mappings.standard_text: Observe and describe how living things (plants and animals, including humans) depend on the natural world to meet their basic needs of food, water, shelter, and air.
mappings.rationale: Dependency and design in creation — Grade 1 entry point for understanding that life systems reflect intentional order.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Why do all living things need the same basic things? Who designed that system?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Observe chickens, garden beds, and rainwater collection as living examples of creation's interdependence on your homestead.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Practical stewardship of the created world

mappings.subject: Social Studies
mappings.standard_text: Identify and explain the purpose of rules and laws in the home, school, and community, and describe how authority figures enforce them.
mappings.rationale: First introduction to delegated authority — connects Biblical concept of governing structures (Romans 13) to everyday civic life.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Who made the rules in your house? Why do we need them?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Discuss property boundaries, water rights, and family 'household laws' as real governance at the homestead scale.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Authority and submission rooted in Scripture

mappings.subject: Health
mappings.standard_text: Identify healthy behaviors that affect personal health, including physical activity, nutrition, sleep, and hygiene.
mappings.rationale: The body as a created system — Grade 1 foundation for treating health as stewardship of God's design, not just medical compliance.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: God made your body to work best when you feed it good food and rest. What does your body need today?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Connect nutrition to growing your own food — garden-to-table meals as a health practice, not just a lifestyle choice.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Food sovereignty and garden medicine

mappings.subject: English Language Arts
mappings.standard_text: Identify the difference between information provided by pictures and information provided by words in an informational text.
mappings.rationale: Pre-critical-thinking entry point — teaching Grade 1 students that pictures and words can tell different stories is the earliest Witness Protocol skill.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Look at this old photograph. What does it show? Now read the caption. Do they match? Which one do you trust more, and why?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Compare seed catalog pictures to actual harvest — do the pictures always tell the whole truth?
mappings.witness_protocol_note: Any PRIMARY_SOURCE block generated from this standard must include at least one evidence entry with similarityScore >= 0.85 before rendering.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Media literacy and source evaluation

mappings.subject: English Language Arts
mappings.standard_text: Ask and answer questions (who, what, where, when, why, and how) to demonstrate understanding of key details in a literary text.
mappings.rationale: The six journalist questions are the foundation of all inquiry — Grade 2 students practice them on stories before applying them to primary sources.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: When you read a story, who is in it? What happens? Where and when does it take place? Why does it matter?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Read a chapter from a classic farm or pioneer story (Little House on the Prairie) and ask the six questions together.

mappings.subject: Mathematics
mappings.standard_text: Understand that the three digits of a three-digit number represent amounts of hundreds, tens, and ones; use place value understanding to read, write, and compare three-digit numbers.
mappings.rationale: Place value is the architecture of our number system — understanding it is understanding how creation counts order at scale.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: If you have 3 hundreds, 2 tens, and 5 ones — what number is that? Why does position matter in math?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Count and bundle dried beans or corn kernels into groups of ones, tens, and hundreds — place value with real produce.

mappings.subject: Science
mappings.standard_text: Plan and conduct an investigation to describe and classify different kinds of materials by their observable properties, including color, texture, hardness, and flexibility.
mappings.rationale: Material properties are the language of creation — God made matter with observable, classifiable, reliable characteristics.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Pick up five things from outside. How are they alike? How are they different? What can you find out just by looking and touching?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Classify soil samples, wood, metal tools, and fiber from the farm — real materials science without a lab.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Knowing material properties guides tool selection and building

mappings.subject: Social Studies
mappings.standard_text: Compare family and community life in the past and present, using photographs, artifacts, and interviews as historical sources.
mappings.rationale: Grade 2 primary source introduction — photographs, artifacts, and interviews as evidence of how things were.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: What did your grandparents' kitchen look like? How do you know? What source could you use to find out?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Interview a grandparent or older neighbor about how they farmed or cooked when they were young. Compare it to how you do it today.

mappings.subject: Health
mappings.standard_text: Identify the benefits of eating a variety of foods from all food groups and explain how food choices affect energy levels and overall health.
mappings.rationale: Nutritional literacy as stewardship — understanding that food variety reflects creation's provision for whole-body health.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Why did God make so many different kinds of food? What does each one do for your body?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Plan a meal using only foods you grew or could grow. What's missing? What would you need to trade for?

neo4j_relationships.reason: Growing food is the most direct form of nutritional control

mappings.subject: English Language Arts
mappings.standard_text: Ask and answer questions to demonstrate understanding of a text, referring explicitly to the text as the basis for the answers.
mappings.rationale: Text-based evidence at Grade 3 — the academic form of the Witness Protocol's requirement to show your source.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Don't just say what you think the text means — show me the exact words that prove it.
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Read a passage from a farming almanac or heritage seed catalog and answer questions using only what the text actually says.

mappings.subject: Mathematics
mappings.standard_text: Interpret products of whole numbers as the total number of objects in equal groups; use multiplication and division within 100 to solve word problems involving equal groups, arrays, and measurement quantities.
mappings.rationale: Multiplication as pattern — God's creation operates in repeatable, predictable structures that multiplication models.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: If you plant 6 rows of corn with 8 seeds in each row, how many seeds did you plant? What pattern are you using?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Calculate how many seeds, fence posts, or animals you need using multiplication — real farm planning with real math.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Planting, harvesting, and selling require multiplication

mappings.subject: Science
mappings.standard_text: Construct an argument that plants and animals have internal and external structures that function to support survival, growth, behavior, and reproduction.
mappings.rationale: Structure and function reflect intentional design — the argument for creation science begins with the observation that structures serve purposes.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Why does a plant have roots? Why does a bird have hollow bones? Could something that useful happen by accident?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Dissect a flower or examine a chicken's wing — identify each part and discuss what it was designed to do.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Understanding plant/animal structures guides better farm practices

mappings.subject: Social Studies
mappings.standard_text: Use primary and secondary sources to describe the contributions of historical figures and communities; distinguish between fact and opinion in historical accounts.
mappings.rationale: Fact vs. opinion in historical sources — the explicit Grade 3 introduction to what the Witness Protocol does at a technical level.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Is this sentence a fact or an opinion? How do you know? What source would you need to prove it's true?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Find two different accounts of the same local historical event (land settlement, drought, or town founding) and compare them.
mappings.witness_protocol_note: Any PRIMARY_SOURCE block must have evidence with similarityScore >= 0.85. Fact/opinion distinctions are central to this standard.

mappings.subject: Social Studies
mappings.standard_text: Identify ways people earn, save, spend, and donate money; explain how making choices about money involves trade-offs.
mappings.rationale: Economic stewardship at Grade 3 — earning, saving, giving, and spending are four dimensions of Biblical faithfulness with money.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: When you earn money for chores, what do you do with it? What if you could only pick one thing?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Set up a simple farm stand. Track what you earned, what you saved, what you spent on seeds, and what you gave away.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Selling handmade or homegrown goods is entrepreneurship
Biblical generosity (tithing, giving) starts with understanding money

mappings.subject: English Language Arts
mappings.standard_text: Refer to details and examples in a text when explaining what the text says explicitly and when drawing inferences from the text.
mappings.rationale: Inference from evidence — Grade 4 students learn to go beyond what is stated to what is implied, the foundation of interpretive reading.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: The author didn't say it directly, but what do you think they meant? What in the text makes you think that?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Read a heritage seed catalog or an old agricultural journal entry. What can you infer about the writer's world that they didn't state outright?

mappings.subject: Mathematics
mappings.standard_text: Recognize that in a multi-digit number, a digit in one place represents ten times what it represents in the place to its right; read and write multi-digit whole numbers using standard form, word form, and expanded form.
mappings.rationale: The structure of large numbers — understanding that land acreage, harvest weights, and business income all require multi-digit fluency.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: If your farm produced 2,450 pounds of corn, how would you write that in words? In expanded form? Why does each digit matter?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Look up the price of neighboring farmland, livestock at auction, or grain storage fees. Read and interpret multi-digit numbers in context.

mappings.subject: Science
mappings.standard_text: Construct an explanation that explains how energy and matter cycle through ecosystems, and how organisms in an ecosystem are interdependent.
mappings.rationale: Ecosystems as designed systems — the cycling of matter and energy demonstrates the elegance and intentionality of creation's economy.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Nothing in God's creation is wasted — how does the same nitrogen that was in a leaf end up back in the soil and then in your food?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Start a compost pile and track what goes in and what comes out. Trace the cycle of nutrients from kitchen scraps back to the garden.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Composting, crop rotation, and soil building are applied ecosystem science

mappings.subject: Social Studies
mappings.standard_text: Examine primary and secondary sources to describe the causes and effects of significant events in Oklahoma and American history, including territorial expansion, Native American displacement, and statehood.
mappings.rationale: Oklahoma history through primary sources — territorial expansion, the Land Run, and Native displacement are examined with actual documents, not textbook summaries.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: What happened to the people who already lived on the Oklahoma land before the Land Run? How do we know? What documents exist?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Research the original land grant or deed for your family's land — who owned it before? What do the records show?
mappings.witness_protocol_note: Claims about Native displacement and territorial history require verified primary source evidence. HistorianAgent must enforce TRUTH_THRESHOLD = 0.85.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Understanding historical injustice is prerequisite to just action today

mappings.subject: Health
mappings.standard_text: Describe how sleep, nutrition, physical activity, and stress management affect the body's ability to function, and identify strategies for maintaining balance in each area.
mappings.rationale: Whole-body stewardship at Grade 4 — the four pillars of natural health connect directly to the body as God's temple.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: When you didn't sleep enough, how did your body feel? How does God's design for sleep help you think and grow?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Track sleep, outdoor activity, and fresh food intake for one week. What patterns do you notice in your energy and mood?

mappings.subject: English Language Arts
mappings.standard_text: Quote accurately from a text and explain what the text says explicitly and what is inferred; summarize the text distinct from personal opinion or judgment.
mappings.rationale: Quoting accurately is the Grade 5 equivalent of the Witness Protocol — you must cite your source exactly, not paraphrase or add opinion.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: What exactly did the author say? Not what you think they meant — the actual words. Now quote them.
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Summarize a chapter of a pioneer memoir (like Willa Cather's O Pioneers!) without adding your own opinion. What did the author actually say?

mappings.subject: Mathematics
mappings.standard_text: Explain patterns in the number of zeros of the product when multiplying a number by powers of ten; explain patterns in the placement of the decimal point when a decimal is multiplied or divided by a power of ten.
mappings.rationale: Decimal precision matters in commerce — pricing crops per pound, calculating yields per acre, and managing accounts all require decimal fluency.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: If wheat sells for $4.75 per bushel, how much do you earn for 100 bushels? For 1,000? What pattern do you see?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Calculate the cost of animal feed, fertilizer, or produce per unit. Practice moving the decimal as quantities scale up and down.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Decimal arithmetic underpins all commerce and budgeting

mappings.subject: Science
mappings.standard_text: Represent data in graphical displays to reveal patterns of daily changes in the length and direction of shadows, day and night, and the seasonal appearance of some stars in the night sky.
mappings.rationale: The heavens declare the glory of God — daily and seasonal patterns in the sky are observable evidence of creation's order.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Why does your shadow change length throughout the day? What is moving — you or the sun? What does the pattern tell you about how God ordered the heavens?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Track shadow length at the same spot every hour for one day. Graph it. Do the same at different seasons and compare.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Planting calendars, harvest timing, and animal cycles follow celestial patterns

mappings.subject: Social Studies
mappings.standard_text: Analyze multiple primary and secondary sources to explain how major events in American history (exploration, colonization, revolution) were caused, experienced, and interpreted differently by various groups.
mappings.rationale: Multiple perspectives on the same event — the advanced form of the Witness Protocol at Grade 5, where different witnesses tell different stories.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: The colonists said it was a revolution for freedom. The Loyalists said it was illegal rebellion. The enslaved said it changed nothing for them. Which account is true? Can all three be?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Find a historical account of westward expansion written by a settler and one written by a Native American from the same era. Compare what each witnessed.
mappings.witness_protocol_note: Multi-perspective history requires PRIMARY_SOURCE evidence from each group represented. HistorianAgent must seek verified sources for each viewpoint.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Understanding who was left out of 'freedom' is prerequisite to justice work

mappings.subject: Social Studies
mappings.standard_text: Explain the concepts of supply and demand, identify how they interact to determine prices, and describe how entrepreneurs respond to market signals.
mappings.rationale: Supply, demand, and entrepreneurship at Grade 5 — the student as a maker and seller, not just a consumer.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: If everyone in town is selling the same thing, what happens to the price? What if you're the only one selling it? How does a maker use that knowledge?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Plan what to plant next season based on what sold well at your farm stand and what your neighbors are growing. That's responding to market signals.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Markets and pricing connect to government policy and regulation
Farm economics is applied supply and demand

mappings.subject: English Language Arts
mappings.standard_text: Cite textual evidence to support analysis of what a text says explicitly as well as inferences drawn from the text; determine the central idea of a text and how it is conveyed through particular details.
mappings.rationale: Formal citation of textual evidence at Grade 6 — the academic equivalent of the Witness Protocol's evidence citation requirement.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: You have an argument. Now prove it from the text. Quote the exact passage, cite where it is, and explain why it supports your claim.
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Read a chapter of a historical agricultural report or government land survey and identify the central claim. What evidence does the author use?

mappings.subject: Mathematics
mappings.standard_text: Understand the concept of a ratio and use ratio language to describe the ratio relationship between two quantities; compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions.
mappings.rationale: Ratios are the mathematics of proportion — soil amendments, feed rations, recipe scaling, and business margins all require ratio reasoning.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: If you mix 2 parts compost to 5 parts soil, what ratio is that? If you have 35 pounds of soil, how much compost do you need?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Mix a potting soil blend, make a fermented feed ratio, or calculate a soap recipe using exact ratios. Real proportional reasoning.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Feed rations, fertilizer mixing, and recipe scaling all use ratios

mappings.subject: Science
mappings.standard_text: Analyze and interpret data to provide evidence that living things are made of cells; distinguish between plant and animal cells and describe the function of major cell organelles.
mappings.rationale: The cell as the fundamental unit of created life — the microscopic reveals the same design principles as the macroscopic.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Every living thing — from an ant to a whale to you — is made of cells following the same basic plan. What does that tell you about who designed life?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Examine onion skin cells under a microscope (or magnifying glass). Compare a plant cell to an animal cell and identify the parts you can see.

mappings.subject: Social Studies
mappings.standard_text: Analyze primary and secondary sources from ancient and medieval civilizations to explain how geography, religion, and economics shaped the development of societies.
mappings.rationale: World history through primary sources — how geography and faith shaped the ancient world is examined with actual documents, maps, and records.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Why did great civilizations always develop near rivers? What primary sources tell us about how they organized themselves?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Map how your region's geography (rivers, soil type, climate) shaped what families farmed and how they traded historically.

mappings.subject: Health
mappings.standard_text: Analyze the relationship between mental and emotional health and physical health; identify healthy coping strategies for managing stress, anxiety, and difficult emotions.
mappings.rationale: Mental and emotional health as whole-person stewardship — the mind-body connection reflects the integrated design of the imago Dei.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: When you're anxious, how does your body feel? What does Scripture say about peace, and how does that connect to physical health?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Identify which farm activities help you feel calm or focused. How does outdoor work, tending animals, or quiet tasks affect your mood and stress?

neo4j_relationships.reason: Biblical peace (Philippians 4:6-7) is both a spiritual and physiological reality

mappings.subject: English Language Arts
mappings.standard_text: Cite several pieces of textual evidence to support analysis of what a text says explicitly as well as inferences drawn from the text; trace and evaluate the argument and specific claims in a text.
mappings.rationale: Multiple citation and argument tracing at Grade 7 — one source is no longer enough. Claims must be corroborated by several pieces of evidence.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: One source says it. Two sources say it. Three sources say it. Now you're building a case — not just an opinion.
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Find three historical sources that describe the same farming practice from different eras. Trace how the argument about best practices changed over time.

mappings.subject: Mathematics
mappings.standard_text: Compute unit rates associated with ratios of fractions; recognize and represent proportional relationships between quantities; decide whether two quantities are in a proportional relationship.
mappings.rationale: Proportional reasoning is the mathematics of justice and commerce — fair pricing, equitable exchange, and accurate measurement all depend on it.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Is this a fair trade? If I give you 3 jars of honey for 2 pounds of beef, is that proportional to last week's deal? How do you check?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Calculate whether a bulk seed purchase is proportionally cheaper than buying small packets. Use unit rates to compare prices per ounce.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Fair pricing and proportional taxation both require ratio reasoning

mappings.subject: Science
mappings.standard_text: Develop a model to describe the cycling of water through Earth's systems (hydrosphere, atmosphere, biosphere, geosphere); explain how human activities affect the water cycle.
mappings.rationale: The water cycle as a designed system — understanding it is prerequisite to water harvesting, drought preparation, and faithful land stewardship.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Where does your well water come from? Where does rainwater go after it hits your land? How does your land management affect that cycle?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Map the water flow on your property — where rain lands, where it runs off, where it soaks in. Design a rainwater harvesting or greywater reuse system.

neo4j_relationships.reason: The water cycle is a designed global system maintained by God (Job 38:25-27)

mappings.subject: Social Studies
mappings.standard_text: Analyze primary and secondary sources to explain patterns of global trade, colonialism, and cultural exchange from 1400–1800, including the Atlantic slave trade and its impact on Africa, Europe, and the Americas.
mappings.rationale: Global history through primary sources — the Atlantic trade network, including the slave trade, is examined through actual ship logs, letters, and government records.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: The slave trade was legal, organized, and profitable. What documents exist that show exactly how it worked? What did the people running it write down?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Research what crops your region produced that were connected to enslaved labor — cotton, tobacco, indigo. Trace the economic chain from your soil to the global market.
mappings.witness_protocol_note: Claims about the Atlantic slave trade must be grounded in verified primary sources (ship manifests, abolition tracts, legal records). HistorianAgent enforces TRUTH_THRESHOLD = 0.85.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Understanding how the slave trade was constructed is prerequisite to understanding abolitionist strategy

mappings.subject: Health
mappings.standard_text: Analyze the influence of food marketing, food deserts, and food systems on individual and community health; develop strategies for making informed, health-supporting food choices within real-world constraints.
mappings.rationale: Food systems literacy at Grade 7 — understanding who controls the food supply is prerequisite to genuine food sovereignty.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Why do the cheapest foods at the grocery store tend to be the least nutritious? Who benefits from that arrangement?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Calculate the true cost of a processed meal vs. a homegrown meal. Include time, seeds, and labor. Which is actually cheaper? Which is actually healthier?

neo4j_relationships.reason: Agricultural subsidies and food policy directly shape what's cheap and available
Growing your own food is the most direct response to food system problems

mappings.subject: English Language Arts
mappings.standard_text: Analyze how the author acknowledges and responds to conflicting evidence or viewpoints by evaluating whether the reasoning is sound and the evidence is relevant and sufficient.
mappings.rationale: Direct Witness Protocol equivalent in ELA — evaluating evidence sufficiency at Grade 8 is the academic form of the 0.85 threshold.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: An author has one version of an event. A primary source has another. How do you decide which account bears witness to what actually happened?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Compare a government agricultural report to a homesteader's journal from the same era — whose account has more to lose by lying?
mappings.witness_protocol_note: HistorianAgent MUST enforce TRUTH_THRESHOLD = 0.85 on all PRIMARY_SOURCE blocks at this standard. ARCHIVE_SILENT triggers a RESEARCH_MISSION.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Evaluating conflicting narratives is prerequisite to just action

mappings.subject: Science
mappings.standard_text: Examine and interpret data on natural hazards, human impacts on Earth's systems, and evaluate the costs and benefits of resource management strategies.
mappings.rationale: Stewardship science at Grade 8 — land and resource management as a moral and practical responsibility, not merely environmental policy.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: What does faithful stewardship of land look like when erosion, drought, or pollution threaten it?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Design a water conservation plan for your homestead based on local watershed data — real stewardship, not theoretical.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Creation care is the theological root of stewardship science
Resource management involves policy, economics, and property rights

mappings.subject: Social Studies
mappings.standard_text: Analyze the role of citizens in identifying injustice, organizing collective action, and advocating for change within constitutional limits.
mappings.rationale: Biblical justice at Grade 8 — distinguishing righteous advocacy (Micah 6:8) from revolution, within covenant structures of civil order.
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: What makes a protest righteous? What crosses the line? How did abolitionists, suffragists, and civil rights leaders navigate this?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Research local zoning or water rights disputes — how did ordinary families advocate for their land within the law?
mappings.witness_protocol_note: Historical claims about specific movements require PRIMARY_SOURCE evidence. ARCHIVE_SILENT must fire if no verified source >= 0.85.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Justice claims require historical evidence
Prophetic justice is rooted in Biblical discipleship, not ideology

mappings.subject: Social Studies
mappings.standard_text: Explain how scarcity, incentives, and trade-offs influence individual and collective economic decisions, including the role of government in markets.
mappings.rationale: Economic decision-making at Grade 8 — connecting scarcity and trade-off theory to Biblical stewardship and honest weights (Proverbs 11:1).
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: If your family has $200 to spend this month, how do you decide between seeds, livestock feed, and medical supplies? That's economics.
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Build a real household budget for a homestead family — track trade-offs between self-sufficiency investments and immediate needs.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Homestead economy is applied economics
Faithfulness with money is a discipleship issue (Luke 16:10)

mappings.subject: Health
mappings.standard_text: Evaluate the influence of media, technology, and cultural factors on food choices, physical activity, and body image, and develop strategies for making informed health decisions.
mappings.rationale: Critical health literacy at Grade 8 — discerning commercial influence from genuine nutritional wisdom, connecting to the body as God's temple (1 Cor. 6:19).
mappings.adeline_lesson_hook: Who profits when you believe you need that supplement, that diet, or that drug? How do you know when health advice is true?
mappings.homestead_adaptation: Compare a processed food label to a homegrown equivalent — trace the ingredients back to their source and evaluate what you actually know.
mappings.witness_protocol_note: Health claims referencing clinical studies require PRIMARY_SOURCE evidence with witnessCitation including archiveName. No unsourced health claims.

neo4j_relationships.reason: Media influence on health is a historical pattern requiring source evaluation
Caring for the body is an act of stewardship and worship

graphrag_loading_instructions.seed_order: Load Kindergarten (grade=0) nodes first; Grade 8 nodes last — progression edges require lower grades to exist first
